questions.text: In plants, Chlorophyll contains which central element?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Iron is sometimes present in chlorophyll but it is not essential. It helps to absorb light energy and is involved in the electron transport chain.

Magnesium is the central atom in chlorophyll. It is what gives chlorophyll its green color. Magnesium is a very important element for plants, as it is involved in many important processes, including photosynthesis.
Phosphorus is not present in chlorophyll. However, it is an important element for plants, as it is involved in many important processes, including photosynthesis.
Nitrogen is also present in chlorophyll, but in smaller amounts. It is involved in the bonding of the carbon atoms and also helps to stabilize the molecule.

questions.text: The internal surface of ileum has many folds, which exhibits velvety appearance due to the presence of numerous finger like outgrowths which are called as:

options.explanation: Villi are further composed of smaller fragments called microvilli

Intestinal villi are tiny, finger-like projections made up of cells that line the entire length of your small intestine. Villi absorb nutrients from the food you eat and then shuttle them into your bloodstream so they can travel where they're needed.

The ileocecal valve is a sphincter muscle situated at the junction of the ileum (last portion of your small intestine) and the colon (first portion of your large intestine). Its function is to allow digested food materials to pass from the small intestine into your large intestine.

questions.text: Discontinuous feeding is possible because of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Discontinuous feeding is possible because of the stomach because it stores food for a long time. It has three functions: Temporarily store food. Contract and relax to mix and break down food. Produce enzymes and other specialized cells to digest food.
The small intestine is where most of the digestion and nutrient absorption take place. It doesn't control the discontinuity of feeding either
The large intestine primarily absorbs water and electrolytes and forms feces from indigestible remnants of food. It does not directly relate to the discontinuity of feeding.

The esophagus is the tube that carries food from the mouth to the stomach through a process called peristalsis. It is not responsible for discontinuous feeding but is essential for the passage of food.

questions.explanation: Discontinuous feeding is possible because of the stomach because it stores food for a long time. It has three functions: Temporarily store food. Contract and relax to mix and break down food. Produce enzymes and other specialized cells to digest food.

questions.text: All the insectovirous plants are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Heterotrophs are organisms that cannot produce their own food and must obtain it from other organisms.
Autotrophs are organisms that can produce their own food from sunlight, water and carbon dioxide. This process is called photosynthesis.
Decomposers are organisms that break down dead organisms and organic matter. They release nutrients back into the environment, which can then be used by other organisms.
Parasites are organisms that live on or in another organism and obtain their nutrients from that organism.

questions.explanation: All the insectivorous plants are autotrophs but when they capture prey their growth becomes rapid.Insectivorous plants are not parasites or decomposers. They are autotrophs that supplement their diet with insects.

questions.text: End of the leaf is modified to form a hood in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sarracenia purpurea, commonly known as the purple pitcher plant, does not have leaves modified into hoods. Instead, it has specialized pitcher-shaped leaves with a tubular structure that traps insects through a combination of nectar and slippery surfaces.

Drosera intermedia, also known as the spoonleaf sundew, typically has leaves with glandular tentacles covered in sticky secretions to capture insects. However, it does not form a hood at the end of its leaves.

The Venus flytrap is the correct option. It has leaves with specialized lobes that resemble a hinged trap. At the end of each lobe, there are trigger-sensitive bristles that, when touched by an insect, cause the lobes to snap shut, forming a hood-like structure that encloses and digests the trapped prey.

questions.explanation: The correct option is Sarracenia purpurea

questions.text: Pick out the different:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dodder is a parasitic plant that lacks chlorophyll and relies entirely on other plants for nutrients. It doesn't have leaves and cannot photosynthesize.

Sundews have leaves covered in glandular tentacles with sticky secretions. These tentacles capture and digest insects to supplement their nutrient intake. Sundews are not parasitic like dodder; they produce their own energy through photosynthesis.
Venus flytraps have specialized leaves with hinged lobes that can snap shut when triggered by touch, capturing insects. They have a unique mechanism for trapping prey

Pitcher plants have tubular or pitcher-shaped leaves that contain a liquid-filled trap. Insects are lured into the trap by nectar or coloration, and once inside, they are unable to escape and are digested by the plant.

questions.explanation: Dodder is an annual seed bearing parastic vine while all others are insectivorous plants.

questions.text: In which of the following types of animals would you except the digestive tract to be more complex:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Animals with a single opening for both ingestion and egestion known as a "sac-like" digestive system, generally have a less complex digestive tract.These animals often have simpler diets and less efficient digestion.
The digestive system can not have more than two openings. So, this opening is incorrect.

Animals with two separate openings, one for ingestion (mouth) and one for egestion (anus), have a digestive system that is more complex than those with a single opening but less complex than those with multiple openings.This separation allows for more efficient digestion and processing of food.

Animals without any digestive openings, also known as "digestive-absent" organisms, have no digestive tract at all.These animals are typically very simple in structure and absorb nutrients directly through their cell membranes.

questions.explanation: Those animals which have tubular type digestive system are more complex as compared to sac like digestive system.Tube has two openings.

questions.text: How many sites of digestion are present in the digestive system of man?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In humans, digestion occurs at three main sites: a. Mouth: Digestion begins in the mouth with the mechanical and chemical breakdown of food by chewing and the action of saliva containing enzymes like amylase. b. Stomach: The stomach continues the digestive process by mixing food with gastric juices, primarily containing hydrochloric acid and pepsin, which break down proteins. c. Small Intestine: The majority of digestion and nutrient absorption occur in the small intestine. Enzymes from the pancreas and bile from the liver help break down and further digest carbohydrates, fats, and proteins.
There are not four primary sites of digestion in the human digestive system
There are not five primary sites of digestion in the human digestive system
There are not six primary sites of digestion in the human digestive system

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. In humans, digestion occurs at three main sites: a. Mouth: Digestion begins in the mouth with the mechanical and chemical breakdown of food by chewing and the action of saliva containing enzymes like amylase. b. Stomach: The stomach continues the digestive process by mixing food with gastric juices, primarily containing hydrochloric acid and pepsin, which break down proteins. c. Small Intestine: The majority of digestion and nutrient absorption occur in the small intestine. Enzymes from the pancreas and bile from the liver help break down and further digest carbohydrates, fats, and proteins.

questions.text: Churning of the food in the mouth significantly improves the digestibility of food stuff by the enzymes:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mastication refers to the process of chewing food in the mouth. Chewing not only breaks down food into smaller, more manageable pieces but also mixes it with saliva. Saliva contains the enzyme amylase, which begins the digestion of carbohydrates. Chewing and mixing with saliva increase the surface area of the food particles, making it easier for enzymes to act on them.

Peristalsis is the rhythmic contraction and relaxation of muscles in the digestive tract that helps move food along the digestive system. While peristalsis is essential for propelling food through the digestive tract, it doesn't directly improve the digestibility of food by enzymes.

Churning typically occurs in the stomach, where the muscular walls contract and mix food with gastric juices. This action helps break down food into a semi-liquid mixture known as chyme, which is more easily digested by enzymes. Churning is especially important for protein digestion.

Acidity in the stomach is primarily due to the presence of hydrochloric acid. This acidic environment serves multiple purposes, including sterilizing food, denaturing proteins, and activating the enzyme pepsin. The acidic conditions in the stomach are crucial for the digestion of proteins.

questions.explanation: Masticiation increase the surface area of food particles which provide site of action for enzymes and significantly improves the digestivity.

questions.text: The digestion of carbohydrates occurs briefly in _ and largely in the _ respectively.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer.Carbohydrate digestion begins in the mouth, where the enzyme amylase in saliva starts breaking down complex carbohydrates into simpler sugars. However, the majority of carbohydrate digestion takes place in the small intestine, where pancreatic amylase and other enzymes continue the process converting complex sugars into absorbable forms.
Carbohydrate digestion primarily occurs in the mouth and small intestine, not in the stomach. The acidic environment of the stomach is more focused on protein digestion.
The esophagus is not a site of digestion. It's a muscular tube that transports food from the mouth to the stomach.

While some carbohydrate digestion does begin in the mouth with the action of salivary amylase, it is not "largely" digested in the stomach. The stomach's primary role is protein digestion due to the presence of hydrochloric acid and pepsin.

questions.explanation: Oral cavity has amylase enzyme and intestine has amylapsin, lactase and maltase for complete digestion of carbohydrates.

questions.explanation: Since amylase is an enzyme so it is not a physical part of the bolus

questions.text: Oral cavity is aided selection of food by all of the following senses except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sense of smell or olfaction plays a significant role in the selection of food.The aroma of food influences our perception of its taste and can either attract or deter us from consuming it. Smell is crucial in identifying and evaluating the desirability of food.

Visual cues are essential in the selection of food. We often decide whether to eat something based on its appearance, color, and presentation. Attractive visual qualities can make food more appealing.

The sense of sound, or hearing, is not typically directly involved in the selection of food. While certain sounds, such as the sizzling of food cooking, can be enticing, they are not as directly related to the initial assessment of food as other senses.

The sense of taste, or gustation, is closely related to the selection of food. It helps us distinguish between basic taste sensations such as sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami. Taste plays a crucial role in determining whether a food is palatable or not.

questions.explanation: Smell, taste and sight help for selection of food, Sound is not related to food selection.

questions.text: Align the following events performed by oral cavity: 1) Digestion 2) Mastication 3) Lubrication 4) Selection
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Digestion - This typically occurs in the stomach and small intestine, not the oral cavity Lubrication - Saliva produced in the oral cavity aids in lubricating food. Selection - The initial assessment of food happens in the oral cavity through sensory cues. Mastication - Chewing food in the mouth is a critical step in breaking it down into smaller pieces.

Selection - The initial assessment of food happens in the oral cavity through sensory cues. Mastication - Chewing food in the mouth is a critical step in breaking it down into smaller pieces. Digestion - This typically occurs in the stomach and small intestine, not the oral cavity. Lubrication - Saliva produced in the oral cavity aids in lubricating food.

Selection - The initial assessment of food happens in the oral cavity through sensory cues. Lubrication - Saliva produced in the oral cavity aids in lubricating food. Mastication - Chewing food in the mouth is a critical step in breaking it down into smaller pieces. Digestion - This typically occurs in the stomach and small intestine, not the oral cavity.

Selection - The initial assessment of food happens in the oral cavity through sensory cues. Mastication - Chewing food in the mouth is a critical step in breaking it down into smaller pieces. Lubrication - Saliva produced in the oral cavity aids in lubricating food. Digestion - This typically occurs in the stomach and small intestine, not the oral cavity.

questions.explanation: 1-Selection of food, 2- Mastication, 3- Lubrication, 4-Enzymitic digestion of food (Starch) will occur in oral cavity.

questions.text: Main function of NaHCO3 and other salts in buccal cavity is to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The buccal cavity does not perform significant chemical digestion of food. Chemical digestion primarily begins in the stomach and intensifies in the small intestine.

This is the correct answer. Saliva contains sodium bicarbonate and other salts that help maintain a near-neutral pH in the mouth. This neutral pH is important for the proper functioning of enzymes like ptyalin (salivary amylase), which initiate the digestion of carbohydrates.

This is also correct. Sodium bicarbonate in saliva helps create an optimal pH environment for ptyalin (salivary amylase) to work effectively. Ptyalin is an enzyme that begins the digestion of starches into simpler sugars.
The main function of NaHCO3 (sodium bicarbonate) and other salts in the buccal cavity (mouth) is to stabilize the pH and act as a promoter of ptyalin (salivary amylase).

questions.explanation: NaHCO3 is bicarbonate which neutralizes the pH in acidic enviroment.

questions.text: All of the following are influenced by the movement of tongue except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The movement of the tongue does not directly influence nasal openings. Nasal openings are primarily controlled by the muscles and structures in the nasal passages and are not affected by tongue movements.

The movement of the tongue can help in redistributing food particles and saliva within the mouth, but it does not directly impact the process of teeth cleansing. Teeth cleansing is more related to actions like brushing and chewing.

The epiglottis is a flap-like structure in the throat that helps prevent food and liquids from entering the windpipe (trachea) and lungs during swallowing. The movement of the tongue plays a role in pushing food toward the epiglottis during swallowing, ensuring that it goes down the esophagus and not the windpipe.

Peristalsis is the rhythmic muscular contractions that propel food and fluids through the esophagus and digestive tract. The movement of the tongue does not directly influence peristalsis, as peristalsis is regulated by the muscles in the digestive tract itself.

questions.explanation: Peristalsis is related to circular and longitudnal muscles of alimentary which stand alter pushing of food to esophagus during swallowing process and this is inherited by tongue.

questions.text: Peristalsis is the characterstic movement of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Peristalsis is not a characteristic movement of the respiratory tract. In the respiratory tract, air moves in and out through the process of inhalation and exhalation, driven by the expansion and contraction of the chest and diaphragm.

Peristalsis is not a characteristic movement of the reproductive tract either. The reproductive tract includes structures like the uterus and fallopian tubes in females and the vas deferens in males, where peristaltic movements are not typically involved.

This is the correct answer. Peristalsis is the rhythmic, wave-like contraction of muscles in the walls of the digestive tract, particularly in the esophagus, stomach, small intestine, and large intestine. It helps move food and liquids along the digestive system, facilitating digestion and absorption of nutrients.

Peristalsis is not a characteristic movement of the urinary tract. The urinary tract consists of the kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra, and its function is to filter and excrete waste products from the body, primarily through the action of the kidneys and bladder, without peristaltic movements.

questions.explanation: Human digestive tract starts from oral cavity and ends at annus. Food in digestive tract moves downward with the help of peristalsis movement.

questions.text: Which of the following region in alimentary cannal produces no enzyme?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The esophagus is a muscular tube that connects the mouth to the stomach. Its primary function is to transport food from the mouth to the stomach through peristaltic contractions. It does not produce digestive enzymes.

The duodenum is the first part of the small intestine, and it plays a critical role in digestion. While the duodenum itself does not produce digestive enzymes, it receives digestive enzymes from the pancreas and bile from the liver to aid in the digestion of fats, proteins, and carbohydrates.
The stomach produces gastric juices containing hydrochloric acid and digestive enzymes like pepsinogen. These enzymes are essential for the initial digestion of proteins in the stomach.

The jejunum is the second part of the small intestine, and it primarily serves as a site for nutrient absorption rather than enzyme production. Most digestive enzymes have already been secreted and activated in the duodenum.

questions.explanation: Oesophagus is a muscular tubular part which starts peristalsis in alimentary cannals and secretes no enzyme.

The digestion of fats primarily occurs in the small intestine with the help of enzymes like pancreatic lipase, not in the oral cavity.

Cellulose is a complex carbohydrate found in plant cell walls. Humans lack the enzyme necessary to digest cellulose, so it is not broken down in the oral cavity or anywhere else in the human digestive system. It passes through the digestive system largely unchanged and provides dietary fiber.

Polypeptides are chains of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. Protein digestion begins in the stomach, where the enzyme pepsinogen is converted into pepsin, and it continues in the small intestine with enzymes like trypsin and chymotrypsin.

questions.text: Food is diverted into the eoesophagus by:

options.explanation: The glottis is the part of the larynx (voice box) that contains the vocal cords. It is primarily involved in regulating airflow during breathing and vocalization. It does not play a role in diverting food.

The cheeks are the fleshy sides of the face that help keep food in the mouth during chewing and assist in manipulating food for proper chewing and swallowing. While the cheeks help with food manipulation, they do not directly divert food into the esophagus.

The tongue is a muscular organ in the mouth that aids in the manipulation of food, mixing it with saliva, and pushing it to the back of the mouth for swallowing. The tongue assists in the swallowing process but does not divert food away from the airway.

This is the correct answer. The epiglottis is a flap-like structure located at the base of the tongue. Its primary function is to cover the opening of the trachea (windpipe) during swallowing, preventing food and liquids from entering the airway. Instead, it directs food and liquids into the esophagus, ensuring that they reach the stomach without entering the respiratory system.

questions.explanation: The Epiglottis is a leaf-shape flaf in the throat that prevents food from entering the windpipe and the lungs.

Salivary amylase does not convert starch into lactose. Lactose is another disaccharide composed of glucose and galactose, and it is not a product of starch digestion.

questions.text: Which combination of the following food components in humans reaches the stomach undigested?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starches, proteins, and fats are all subject to digestion in different parts of the digestive system. Starches are broken down into simpler sugars in the mouth and small intestine, proteins are digested in the stomach and small intestine, and fats are emulsified and digested primarily in the small intestine. None of these components typically reach the stomach undigested.

Vitamins and carbohydrates are not usually broken down in the stomach but are absorbed in the small intestine. Polypeptides (short protein chains) are further digested into individual amino acids in the stomach and small intestine. So, this combination is not known for reaching the stomach undigested.

Cellulose, a type of dietary fiber, is not digestible by humans because we lack the enzymes needed to break it down. Therefore, cellulose reaches the stomach undigested. Proteins are partially digested in the stomach, and fats are eventually digested in the small intestine, but cellulose remains largely intact and provides bulk to the diet.

Starches are partially digested in the mouth and small intestine, proteins are partially digested in the stomach, and cellulose is not digestible by humans. So, this combination also includes cellulose, which reaches the stomach undigested.

questions.explanation: Oral cavity is the site of digestion of protein, cellulose and fats are not digested.

questions.text: Enzyme pepsin acts on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pepsin is a proteolytic enzyme, meaning it breaks down proteins. It functions in the acidic environment of the stomach, where the stomach lining secretes hydrochloric acid (HCl) to create an acidic environment (pH around 1-2). In this acidic environment, pepsinogen, the inactive precursor of pepsin, is secreted by the chief cells of the gastric glands. The acidic conditions in the stomach convert pepsinogen into its active form, pepsin. Pepsin then acts on dietary proteins, cleaving the peptide bonds between amino acids and breaking the proteins down into smaller peptides.

options.explanation: The hepatic vein is responsible for draining deoxygenated blood from the liver and carrying it back to the vena cava, which is a large vein that returns blood to the heart. The hepatic vein is not part of the hepatic portal system.

questions.text: Which one of the following is endocrine pair present in digestive canal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The esophagus is not an endocrine organ. It is just a tube through which food passes.
The pancreas is part of the endocrine system and the digestive system. That's because it secretes hormones into the bloodstream, and makes and secretes enzymes into the digestive tract.

The stomach and duodenum are not typically considered as an endocrine pair in the digestive canal. While they do play important roles in digestion, their primary functions are related to the secretion of digestive enzymes and the breakdown of food rather than hormone production. The stomach mainly produces gastric acid, enzymes, and mucus to aid in the digestion of food. The primary hormone produced by specialized cells in the stomach is gastrin, which stimulates the secretion of gastric acid. Gastrin acts locally within the stomach to regulate acid production and gastric motility. The duodenum, which is the first part of the small intestine, is involved in receiving partially digested food from the stomach and further breaking it down with the help of bile and pancreatic enzymes. While the duodenum does have endocrine cells, their functions are primarily related to nutrient sensing and regulating pancreatic and gallbladder secretions.
The liver is not an endocrine organ.

questions.explanation: The pancreas and liver are the endocrine pair present in the digestive canal.While the liver is primarily known for its exocrine functions, such as bile production, it also plays a significant role in endocrine function. The liver contains specialized cells called hepatocytes, which produce and secrete important endocrine hormones.One of the key hormones produced by the liver is insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1), which is involved in promoting growth and development in various tissues throughout the body. Additionally, the liver produces and releases other hormones, such as angiotensinogen, thrombopoietin, and hepcidin, which are involved in regulating blood pressure, platelet production, and iron metabolism, respectively. The pancreas, as previously mentioned, has both exocrine and endocrine functions. The endocrine portion of the pancreas consists of the pancreatic islets (islets of Langerhans), which contain different types of endocrine cells. The pancreas secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream to regulate various processes in the body. The two main hormones produced by the endocrine pancreas are insulin and glucagon. Insulin helps regulate blood sugar levels by facilitating the uptake of glucose from the bloodstream into cells, while glucagon acts to increase blood sugar levels by stimulating the release of stored glucose from the liver. So, both the pancreas and the liver have significant endocrine functions within the digestive canal.

questions.text: All principal components of food can be digested by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gastric juice, produced by the gastric glands in the stomach, mainly contains hydrochloric acid and pepsinogen. While gastric juice helps with the digestion of proteins by activating pepsinogen to form pepsin, it does not have enzymes that can fully digest all the principal components of food.
Pancreatic juice contains various digestive enzymes that are capable of digesting all the principal components of food.

Bile is produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder before being released into the small intestine. It primarily aids in the digestion and absorption of fats by emulsifying them, which increases the surface area for enzymes to act upon. However, bile itself does not contain enzymes for the digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, or fats.

While oral secretions, such as saliva, contain enzymes like amylase that initiate the digestion of carbohydrates in the mouth, they do not contain the full range of enzymes required for the digestion of all principal components of food.

questions.explanation: Pancreas has complete set of enzymes for digestion of all principal components (carbohydrates, proteins and lipids) of food. Pancreatic juice contains various digestive enzymes that are capable of digesting all the principal components of food. These enzymes include amylases (for carbohydrate digestion), lipases (for lipid digestion), and proteases (for protein digestion). The pancreas plays a crucial role in producing and secreting these enzymes into the small intestine, where they act on the different components of food.

Blood from the digestive tract contains nutrients, toxins, and other substances that need to be processed before they can be used or removed from the body. The liver filters this blood and removes toxins, excess nutrients, and other harmful substances, converting them into less harmful or more easily excreted compounds.

questions.text: All of the following are lined with mucous membrane of cilated epithelium except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nasal cavity is lined by ciliated epithelium and mucus membrane to help protect the respiratory system from harmful particles and microorganisms. The cilia move in a coordinated manner to help move mucus and trapped particles out of the nasal cavity. The mucus membrane also helps to trap these particles and prevent them from entering the lungs, where they can cause damage and inflammation. Additionally, the nasal cavity is responsible for warming and humidifying the air we breathe, which helps to prevent irritation of the delicate tissues in the respiratory system.

The trachea is lined by ciliated epithelium and mucus membrane to help protect the lungs from harmful particles and microorganisms that may be present in the air we breathe. The cilia, which are tiny hair-like structures on the surface of the cells, move in a coordinated manner to help move mucus and trapped particles out of the trachea and away from the lungs. The mucus membrane also helps to trap these particles and prevent them from reaching the lungs, where they can cause damage and inflammation.

The bronchi, like the trachea, are lined by ciliated epithelium and mucus to help protect the lungs from harmful particles and microorganisms that may be present in the air we breathe. The cilia move in a coordinated manner to help move mucus and trapped particles out of the bronchi and away from the lungs. The mucus membrane also helps to trap these particles and prevent them from reaching the lungs, where they can cause damage and inflammation. This is an important part of the body's defense against respiratory infections and other conditions that can affect the lungs.

Bronchioles are not lined by ciliated epithelium and mucus membrane because they are too small to accommodate the presence of cilia and mucus-secreting cells. The bronchioles are the smallest branches of the airways in the lungs and their diameter is less than 1 mm. Instead, the bronchioles are lined with simple cuboidal epithelium, which does not contain cilia or mucus-secreting cells. The main function of the bronchioles is to regulate the flow of air into the alveoli, where gas exchange occurs.

questions.explanation: Ciliated epithelium containing goblet cells is absent in bronchioles.

questions.text: Which of the following function is not accosiated with Nasal Cavity:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nasal cavity filters the air we breathe. The nasal cavity is lined with fine hairs called cilia, which help to trap particles such as dust, dirt, and pollen as they enter the nasal cavity. The mucus membrane in the nasal cavity also helps to trap these particles and prevent them from entering the lungs, where they can cause damage and inflammation.
The nasal cavity does not significantly change the pH of the air we breathe. The nasal cavity does not have the ability to significantly alter the pH of the air we breathe.

The nasal cavity also moistens the air we breathe. The air that we inhale is typically dry, and if it is too dry, it can irritate the delicate tissues in the respiratory system and cause inflammation or other respiratory problems. The nasal cavity contains a mucus membrane that produces mucus, which helps to moisten the air as it passes through. The mucus also helps to trap particles such as dust, dirt, and pollen, which can cause irritation or other respiratory problems.

The nasal cavity is responsible for warming the air we breathe. When we inhale, the air that enters the nasal cavity is typically cooler than our body temperature. The nasal cavity contains a network of blood vessels, which help to warm the air as it passes through. This is important because cold air can irritate the delicate tissues in the respiratory system and cause inflammation or other respiratory problems.

questions.explanation: The nasal cavity functions to humidify, warm, filter, and act as a conduit for inspired air, as well as protect the respiratory tract through the use of the mucociliary system. The nasal cavity also houses the receptors responsible for olfaction.

options.explanation: The larynx, also known as the voice box, is a cartilaginous structure located in the neck, between the pharynx and the trachea. The larynx is made up of several pieces of cartilage that are held together by ligaments and muscles. The main function of the larynx is to protect the lower airway and to produce sound for speech and singing.

The esophagus is a muscular structure that is part of the digestive system. The esophagus is a long, muscular tube that connects the mouth to the stomach, and its main function is to transport food and liquids from the mouth to the stomach.
The pharynx is a muscular structure that is located at the back of the throat. It plays a vital role in the digestive and respiratory systems.
Epiglottis is a cartilaginous flap which covers the glottis partially during swallowing. Glottis is the opening of trachea.

questions.text: A muscuarly controlled hinge, like action and serves as a lid which automatically covers the opening of the larynx is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The structure you are referring to is called the epiglottis. The epiglottis is a flap of cartilage located at the base of the tongue that helps to prevent food and liquids from entering the trachea and lungs during swallowing. When we swallow, the epiglottis closes over the opening of the larynx, which is the entrance to the trachea, to prevent food and liquids from entering the airway. The epiglottis is an important structure in the respiratory and digestive systems, as it helps to prevent choking and aspiration of food and liquids into the lungs.
The esophageal sphincter is located at the junction between the esophagus and the stomach, and its main function is to prevent stomach contents from flowing back up into the esophag

The uvula is a small, fleshy, bell-shaped structure that hangs down from the soft palate in the back of the mouth. It is made up of muscle fibers, connective tissue, and glandular tissue, and it plays a role in speech and swallowing. The uvula helps to prevent food and liquids from entering the nasal cavity during swallowing by directing them towards the esophagus. It also plays a role in the production of certain speech sounds, particularly in languages that use guttural or pharyngeal consonants.

This function is primarily performed by the epiglottis, which is a flap of cartilage that is located at the base of the tongue and helps to prevent food and liquids from entering the airway during swallowing.

questions.text: Opening from nose to throat is closed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Epiglottis is a cartilaginous flap which covers the glottis partially during swallowing. Glottis is the opening of trachea.

The esophageal sphincter is located at the junction between the esophagus and the stomach, and its main function is to prevent stomach contents from flowing back up into the esophagus. When we swallow, the esophageal sphincter relaxes to allow food and liquids to pass from the esophagus into the stomach. After swallowing, the esophageal sphincter contracts to prevent stomach contents, including acid and digestive enzymes, from flowing back up into the esophagus.

The soft palate is a muscular structure that is located at the back of the roof of the mouth, or the hard palate. It is made up of muscle fibers, connective tissue, and glandular tissue, and it plays an important role in speech, swallowing, and breathing. The soft palate is connected to the hard palate at the front and to the uvula at the back, and it helps to separate the oral cavity from the nasal cavity. During swallowing, the soft palate and uvula move upward and backward to close off the nasopharynx, which is the opening from the nose to the pharynx. This helps to prevent food and liquids from entering the nasal cavity and instead directs them towards the esophagus. The muscles of the pharynx also contract to help push food and liquids down into the esophagus and prevent them from entering the airway.

The tongue is covered in small bumps called papillae, which contain taste buds that allow us to detect different flavors. The tongue also plays an important role in speech, as it helps to shape the sounds that we produce when we speak.During swallowing, the tongue helps to push food and liquids towards the back of the mouth and into the pharynx, which is the muscular tube that connects the mouth to the esophagus. From there, the food and liquids pass through the esophagus and into the stomach.

questions.explanation: The soft palate is a muscular structure that is located at the back of the roof of the mouth, or the hard palate. It is made up of muscle fibers, connective tissue, and glandular tissue, and it plays an important role in speech, swallowing, and breathing. The soft palate is connected to the hard palate at the front and to the uvula at the back, and it helps to separate the oral cavity from the nasal cavity. During swallowing, the soft palate and uvula move upward and backward to close off the nasopharynx, which is the opening from the nose to the pharynx. This helps to prevent food and liquids from entering the nasal cavity and instead directs them towards the esophagus. The muscles of the pharynx also contract to help push food and liquids down into the esophagus and prevent them from entering the airway.

questions.text: A cavity bounded by ribs and diaphragram is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: •The abdominal cavity is lined by the peritoneum, a thin, transparent membrane that covers the inside wall of the cavity (parietal peritoneum) as well as every organ or structure contained within it (visceral peritoneum).•The peritoneum is an important structure that helps to protect the organs within the abdominal cavity and provides a smooth surface for them to move against each other during digestion. It also helps to hold the organs in place and prevent them from shifting or moving around too much. •Diaphragm separates thoracic and abdominal cavity.

The cavity bounded by the ribs and diaphragm is called the thoracic cavity, or chest cavity. It is a large, hollow space that contains the heart, lungs, major blood vessels, and other important structures of the respiratory and cardiovascular systems. The thoracic cavity is separated from the abdominal cavity by the diaphragm, which is a thin sheet of muscle that helps to control breathing by contracting and relaxing.The thoracic cavity is essential for breathing, as it provides a protected space for the lungs to expand and contract as we inhale and exhale. It also helps to protect the heart and other vital organs from injury or damage.

The pelvic cavity is a different cavity from the thoracic cavity. The pelvic cavity is located in the lower part of the torso and is bounded by the pelvic bones. It contains the bladder, reproductive organs, and rectum, among other structures.

The pleural cavity is enclosed by two layers of pleura, which is a thin, slippery membrane that surrounds the lungs and lines the chest cavity. The pleural cavity is a small, fluid-filled space that exists between the two layers of pleura.

questions.text: Bronchioles are made up of mainly:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While bronchioles do contain some connective tissues, they are not the main component of bronchioles. Connective tissues provide structural support and form the framework for blood vessels and nerves, but they are not the predominant tissue type in bronchioles.

Goblet cells are specialized cells found in the respiratory system, including the bronchioles. However, they are not the main component of bronchioles. Goblet cells produce and secrete mucus, but they are not the primary structural component that makes up bronchioles.

The walls of bronchioles contain circular smooth muscles, which are responsible for regulating the diameter of the airways. These muscles can contract and relax, allowing the bronchioles to constrict or dilate, thereby controlling the airflow.

Endothelial cells line the inner surface of blood vessels, including the capillaries within the bronchioles. While endothelial cells are present in bronchioles, they are not the primary tissue that comprises bronchioles.

questions.text: Each air sac consists of several microscopic alveoli which are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The walls of alveoli are made up of a thin layer of squamous epithelial cells, which are simple and flattened cells forming a single layer. These type I alveolar cells are extremely thin to facilitate efficient gas exchange between the alveoli and the surrounding capillaries.

Alveoli are small, thin-walled sacs within the lungs where gas exchange occurs between the air and the bloodstream. They are essential for the process of respiration, where oxygen from the air is taken up by the blood, and carbon dioxide is released from the blood into the air. Alveoli are composed of a single layer of cells. The walls of alveoli are made up of a thin layer of squamous epithelial cells, which are simple and flattened cells forming a single layer. These type I alveolar cells are extremely thin to facilitate efficient gas exchange between the alveoli and the surrounding capillaries. Type II alveolar cells (septal cells) are also present, as mentioned earlier, but they are scattered among the type I cells. Type II cells are responsible for producing surfactant, which helps prevent the alveoli from collapsing during exhalation and ensures proper lung function.

The walls of alveoli are made up of a thin layer of squamous epithelial cells, which are simple and flattened cells forming a single layer. These type I alveolar cells are extremely thin to facilitate efficient gas exchange between the alveoli and the surrounding capillaries.

The alveolar epithelium comprises two main cell types: the alveolar type I and alveolar type II cell. The type I cell is a complex branched cell with multiple cytoplasmic plates that are greatly attenuated and relatively devoid of organelles; these plates represent the gas exchange surface in the alveolus.

questions.explanation: Alveoli are microscopic balloon-shaped structures located at the end of the respiratory tree. They expand during inhalation, taking in oxygen, and shrink during exhalation, expelling carbon dioxide. These tiny air sacs are the site where gas exchange between inspired air and the blood takes place.

questions.text: The function of nasal cavity is:

The nasal cavity is a part of the respiratory system located behind the nose. It performs three important functions: Moistening: The nasal cavity produces mucus to moisten the air we breathe, preventing the respiratory tract from drying out. Protecting: Mucus in the nasal cavity traps dust, allergens, and other particles, keeping them from reaching the lungs. Regulating Temperature: The nasal cavity warms the inhaled air to body temperature, preventing discomfort from breathing in cold air. These functions are crucial for the proper functioning and protection of the respiratory system.

options.explanation: Ciliated epithelium lines the human respiratory tract from the posterior third of the nose to the bronchioles. In humans, cilia are present in the paranasal sinuses, the Eustachian tubes, the ependymal lining of the central nervous system, in the male vasa efferentia, and the female oviducts, among other structures.

The cells lining the alveoli are known as squamous epithelial cells or type I alveolar cells. These cells are extremely thin and flat, and they form a single layer that makes up the alveolar wall. The structure of the squamous epithelial cells in the alveoli is specifically adapted to facilitate efficient gas exchange. Their thinness allows for easy diffusion of gases (oxygen and carbon dioxide) between the alveoli and the surrounding capillaries.

Cuboidal epithelial cells are a type of epithelial tissue characterized by their cube-like or hexagonal shape when viewed under a microscope. These cells have roughly equal height, width, and depth, giving them a three-dimensional appearance.

Columnar epithelial cells are another type of epithelial tissue, and as the name suggests, they have a tall, elongated shape, resembling columns. These cells are taller than they are wide and often have a nucleus located near the base of the cell.

questions.explanation: The alveolus is composed of alveolar epithelial type 1 cells, alveolar epithelial type 2 cells and capillary cells. Type 1 cells are large, squamous epithelial cells that cover ~95% of the alveolar surface area. Type 2 cells produce surfactant and play a role in maintenance of lung fluid balance.

questions.text: How many functional pairs of vocal cords are present in humans:

options.explanation: Humans have two functional pairs of vocal cords, which are also known as vocal folds. These vocal cords are located within the larynx (voice box) in the throat.
Humans have two functional pairs of vocal cords, which are also known as vocal folds. These vocal cords are located within the larynx (voice box) in the throat.

Humans have two functional pairs of vocal cords, which are also known as vocal folds. These vocal cords are located within the larynx (voice box) in the throat. Together, these two functional pairs of vocal cords work harmoniously to produce sound and enable communication through speech and singing. The pitch and tone of the sound are controlled by adjusting the tension and position of the vocal cords, while the shape of the vocal tract (mouth, nose, and throat) modifies the sound into different speech sounds and melodies.

"Ventral" is a term used in anatomy to describe a directional orientation or position relative to the body. It refers to the front or anterior side of an organism or a specific body part. The trachea lies in front of the esophagus. The trachea, also known as the windpipe, is a tube-like structure that carries air to and from the lungs, facilitating the process of breathing. questions.text: ---- are folds of tissue within the larynx, creates sounds when vibrate.

options.explanation: Vocal cords, also known as vocal folds, are a pair of flexible, fold-like structures located in the larynx (voice box) within the throat. They are composed of specialized tissue and muscle and play a crucial role in producing sound and enabling speech and singing. When you speak or sing, air from the lungs passes through the trachea (windpipe) and into the larynx. As the air flows through the vocal cords, they come together, causing them to vibrate. These vibrations produce sound waves, which, when modified by the shape of the vocal tract (mouth, nose, and throat), result in various speech sounds and tones.

The epiglottis is a leaf-shaped, cartilaginous flap-like structure located at the base of the tongue, in the throat. Its primary function is to prevent food and liquids from entering the windpipe (trachea) and the lower respiratory tract during swallowing. When we swallow, the epiglottis folds downward, covering the entrance to the larynx (voice box) and trachea, directing food and liquids toward the esophagus and digestive system instead. This action ensures that the airway remains protected and clear during the process of swallowing.

The glottis is the space or opening between the vocal cords in the larynx (voice box). It is the part of the larynx where the vocal cords are located, and it plays a crucial role in sound production during speech and singing.

The trachea, also known as the windpipe, is a tube-like structure that extends from the larynx down into the chest. It is a vital part of the respiratory system, responsible for conducting air to and from the lungs.

Nasopharynx: Located behind the nasal cavity, it serves as a passageway for air from the nose into the respiratory system. Oropharynx: Situated behind the mouth, it serves as a passageway for both air and swallowed food or liquids. Laryngopharynx: Located behind the larynx (voice box), it serves as a passageway for both air and swallowed food or liquids to their respective destinations - the trachea and esophagus.
The human pharynx is divided into 3 sections: oropharynx, nasopharynx and laryngopharynx.

questions.text: The respiratory bronchioles terminate in elongated airways called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Alveolar sacs are clusters or groups of alveoli that resemble bunches of grapes. Each alveolar sac is connected to several alveolar ducts and serves as a common space for multiple alveoli. This arrangement increases the surface area available for gas exchange in the lungs, allowing for efficient oxygenation of the blood and removal of carbon dioxide.
Bronchi divide into bronchioles, not the other way around.

Alveolar ducts are tiny, thin-walled air passages that follow the respiratory bronchioles in the lungs. They are part of the airway network that delivers air deep into the lungs. The alveolar ducts are lined with simple squamous epithelial cells and are surrounded by networks of capillaries. They serve as conduits for air to reach the alveoli, where gas exchange occurs.

Alveoli are tiny, balloon-like air sacs found at the end of the respiratory bronchioles and alveolar ducts in the lungs. They are the primary sites of gas exchange in the respiratory system. The alveoli are surrounded by an extensive network of pulmonary capillaries. Oxygen from the inhaled air diffuses across the thin walls of the alveoli into the bloodstream, and carbon dioxide, a waste product, diffuses from the bloodstream into the alveoli to be exhaled from the body.

The cartilages in the trachea, also known as tracheal cartilages or tracheal rings, are C-shaped cartilaginous structures that provide support and maintain the patency of the trachea (windpipe). There are about 16 to 20 tracheal cartilages in the trachea, and they are composed of hyaline cartilage. These C-shaped cartilages form the anterior (front) and lateral (side) walls of the trachea, while the posterior (back) wall lacks cartilage. This design allows the trachea to be flexible and expandable, ensuring smooth airflow during breathing.
There is no mention of "D-shaped" cartilages in the human respiratory system.

The G-shaped cartilage refers to the cricoid cartilage, which is the only complete ring of cartilage in the larynx (voice box). The cricoid cartilage is located just below the thyroid cartilage (Adam's apple) and sits on top of the trachea. It forms the lowermost part of the larynx and provides structural support to the larynx, ensuring the airway remains open during respiration.

questions.explanation: C-shaped cartilaginous rings reinforces the anterior and lateral sides of the trachea to protect and maintain the airway open. (The cartilaginous rings are incomplete because this allows the trachea to collapse slightly to allow food to pass down the esophagus.)

questions.text: In humans, the respiratory centre is:

options.explanation: The cerebrum is responsible for many higher-order brain functions, including consciousness, perception, voluntary movement, thinking, reasoning, problem-solving, language processing, and sensory processing. It also plays a critical role in memory formation, learning, and emotions.

The medulla oblongata is a vital part of the brainstem located just above the spinal cord. It plays a critical role in regulating essential involuntary functions necessary for survival, including the control of respiration. In the medulla oblongata, there are clusters of neurons known as the medullary respiratory centers. These centers are responsible for controlling the rhythm and rate of breathing.

The pons is a region of the brainstem located above the medulla oblongata and below the midbrain. It is an important part of the brainstem that serves as a bridge between the cerebrum and the cerebellum, as well as between the cerebrum and the rest of the brain and spinal cord.

The midbrain is involved in various functions, including visual and auditory processing, reflex actions, and regulation of movement. The tectum contains the superior colliculi, which play a role in visual processing, and the inferior colliculi, which are involved in auditory processing.

It is a mixture of lipids (fats) and proteins produced and secreted by specialized cells called type II alveolar cells, also known as type II pneumocytes. Surfactant is a mixture of lipids and proteins produced by lung cells. It reduces surface tension in the alveoli, preventing their collapse during exhalation, ensuring efficient gas exchange in the lungs, and supporting proper lung development, especially in premature infants.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a function of surfactant?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Surfactant is not involved in the filtration of blood. in fact, it does not come in contact with blood at all.

By reducing surface tension and preventing alveolar collapse, surfactant ensures efficient gas exchange in the lungs. During inhalation, oxygen from the inhaled air diffuses across the alveolar walls and into the bloodstream, where it is transported to various tissues in the body. Similarly, carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism, is exchanged from the bloodstream into the alveoli to be exhaled during exhalation. The presence of surfactant ensures that these gas exchange processes occur smoothly and effectively.

Surfactant is crucial for reducing the surface tension at the air-liquid interface within the alveoli of the lungs. The inner lining of the alveoli is coated with a thin layer of fluid, and the surface tension of this fluid tends to cause the alveoli to collapse during exhalation. Surfactant molecules interact with the water molecules in the fluid and help to break down the surface tension. This action prevents the alveoli from collapsing and allows them to remain open, facilitating efficient and easier breathing.
Surfactant acts like an antispetic because it reduces the chances of an infection.

questions.explanation: Surfactants is a lipoprotein layer inside alveoli, which reduce surface tension between water molecules to increase gases exchange.

questions.text: Which energy is consumed in breathing?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During breathing, chemical energy in the form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is consumed. ATP is the primary energy currency in living cells, and it is used to power various cellular processes, including muscle contraction, active transport of ions, and biosynthetic reactions.Breathing involves the contraction and relaxation of respiratory muscles, primarily the diaphragm and intercostal muscles, to expand and contract the chest cavity. These muscle movements require energy to perform mechanical work.

Physical energy refers to the total energy present in a physical system. It encompasses all forms of energy that can exist within a system, including potential energy, kinetic energy, thermal energy, chemical energy, and nuclear energy. Physical energy is a broad term that encompasses all types of energy and their interconversion within a system.

Potential energy is a form of energy that an object possesses due to its position or configuration relative to other objects. It is the energy that is stored in an object and has the potential to be converted into other forms of energy and do work. The potential energy of an object depends on factors such as its mass, height, and the force acting on it.

Mechanical energy is a specific form of energy associated with the motion and position of an object. It is the sum of an object's kinetic energy (energy of motion) and potential energy (energy due to its position). In the absence of non-conservative forces like friction or air resistance, the total mechanical energy of a system remains constant.

questions.explanation: Over all process of breathing is active and consumes energy during inspiration in the form of ATP

questions.text: Partial pressure of oxygen is maximum in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When you inhale, you bring air into your lungs. Inhaled air typically contains about 21% oxygen, which is the same percentage as the oxygen concentration in the Earth's atmosphere. The majority of inhaled air consists of nitrogen (about 78%) and a small percentage of other gases like carbon dioxide (around 0.04%) and trace amounts of other gases.

Alveolar air refers to the air found in the alveoli of the lungs. After the exchange of gases between the air in the alveoli and the bloodstream, alveolar air typically contains a lower concentration of oxygen than atmospheric air. The reason for this decrease is that oxygen from the alveolar air diffuses into the bloodstream, where it binds to hemoglobin in red blood cells for transport throughout the body. As a result, the percentage of oxygen in alveolar air is usually around 13-15%.

When you exhale, the air that leaves your lungs has a different composition compared to inhaled air. Exhaled air contains less oxygen and more carbon dioxide (CO2) than inhaled air. Exhaled air typically has around 16-17% oxygen and an increased concentration of carbon dioxide, about 4-5%.

Oxygenated blood is the blood that has been enriched with oxygen after passing through the lungs. As blood flows through the pulmonary capillaries, it picks up oxygen from the alveolar air in the alveoli. The oxygen molecules bind to hemoglobin in red blood cells, forming oxyhemoglobin. Oxygenated blood leaving the lungs and returning to the heart for distribution to the body's tissues typically has a higher oxygen concentration than alveolar air. The percentage of oxygen in oxygenated arterial blood is usually around 95-100%.

questions.explanation: Oxygen is 21% in inhaled airand it is 16% in exhaled air.

questions.text: Which of the following factor does not alter the rate of breathing by influencing the chemoreceptors in medulla oblongata, aorta and carotid artery?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: CO2 partial pressures in the blood
options.explanation: The rate of breathing is primarily regulated by the respiratory centers in the medulla oblongata and pons, which respond to chemical signals from the chemoreceptors in the aorta and carotid arteries. These chemoreceptors are sensitive to changes in the levels of carbon dioxide (CO2) partial pressure, hydrogen ion (H+) concentration (pH), and oxygen (O2) partial pressure in the blood.

The rate of breathing is primarily regulated by the respiratory centers in the medulla oblongata and pons, which respond to chemical signals from the chemoreceptors in the aorta and carotid arteries. These chemoreceptors are sensitive to changes in the levels of carbon dioxide (CO2) partial pressure, hydrogen ion (H+) concentration (pH), and oxygen (O2) partial pressure in the blood.

options.text: O2 partial pressures in the blood
options.explanation: The rate of breathing is primarily regulated by the respiratory centers in the medulla oblongata and pons, which respond to chemical signals from the chemoreceptors in the aorta and carotid arteries. These chemoreceptors are sensitive to changes in the levels of carbon dioxide (CO2) partial pressure, hydrogen ion (H+) concentration (pH), and oxygen (O2) partial pressure in the blood.

•The rate of breathing is primarily regulated by the respiratory centers in the medulla oblongata and pons, which respond to chemical signals from the chemoreceptors in the aorta and carotid arteries. These chemoreceptors are sensitive to changes in the levels of carbon dioxide (CO2) partial pressure, hydrogen ion (H+) concentration (pH), and oxygen (O2) partial pressure in the blood. •Blood glucose level, on the other hand, does not directly influence the rate of breathing through the chemoreceptors in the medulla oblongata, aorta, and carotid artery. Instead, blood glucose levels are primarily regulated by hormones such as insulin and glucagon, and they play a crucial role in maintaining glucose homeostasis in the body. However, glucose levels can indirectly affect breathing in cases of severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia, which may lead to altered levels of consciousness or metabolic imbalances that can impact respiratory function. But it is not a direct regulator of the rate of breathing through the chemoreceptors mentioned.

questions.text: If we hold our breath for a long time, CO2 levels are likely to and the pH of body is fluids is likely to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When we hold our breath or reduce our breathing rate significantly, the body's ability to eliminate CO2 decreases, leading to its accumulation in the blood. As CO2 dissolves in the blood, it forms carbonic acid (H2CO3), which dissociates into bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and hydrogen ions (H+). This increased production of hydrogen ions leads to a decrease in the pH of body fluids, making them more acidic.

questions.text: During expiration the diaphragm becomes:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During expiration, the diaphragm and other respiratory muscles relax. As a result, the diaphragm moves upward passively due to the elastic recoil of the lung tissues and the relaxation of the surrounding muscles. This upward movement reduces the volume of the thoracic cavity, causing an increase in pressure within the lungs. This increased pressure helps to expel air from the lungs.The dome-shaped relaxation of the diaphragm during expiration allows the thoracic cavity to decrease in volume effectively, aiding in the expulsion of air.

questions.text: During inspiration the diaphragm:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During inhalation, the diaphragm, which is a dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity, contracts and flattens. This contraction causes the diaphragm to move downward, which increases the volume of the thoracic cavity. As the thoracic cavity expands, it creates a negative pressure within the lungs, and air is drawn into the lungs from the outside to equalize the pressure. This is how the process of inhalation occurs, and the diaphragm plays a vital role in this process by creating the necessary pressure difference.

questions.text: The low levels of surfactant produced by alveolar epithelium causes:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Emphysema is characterized by the destruction of the alveolar walls, leading to decreased lung elasticity and increased air trapping.

Surfactant is a substance produced by the alveolar epithelium in the lungs, and it plays a crucial role in reducing surface tension within the alveoli (small air sacs in the lungs). This reduces the effort required to expand the alveoli during inhalation and prevents their collapse during exhalation. Surfactant is particularly important for the proper functioning of the lungs, especially in premature infants whose lungs may not have produced sufficient surfactant at birth.Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) occurs primarily in premature infants who have not yet produced enough surfactant in their lungs. As a result, their lungs have higher surface tension, making it difficult for them to expand and fill with air properly. This leads to respiratory difficulty, shallow breathing, and can result in severe breathing problems and insufficient oxygenation of the blood.
Bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchial tubes, and asthma involves chronic inflammation and constriction of the airways.

Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition characterized by inflammation and narrowing of the airways, leading to recurrent episodes of wheezing, coughing, and shortness of breath. It is often triggered by various factors such as allergens, respiratory infections, exercise, or cold air.

questions.explanation: Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) occurs primarily in premature infants who have not yet produced enough surfactant in their lungs. As a result, their lungs have higher surface tension, making it difficult for them to expand and fill with air properly. This leads to respiratory difficulty, shallow breathing, and can result in severe breathing problems and insufficient oxygenation of the blood.

questions.text: Diffusion of respiratory gases is inversely proportional to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Diffusion of respiratory gases is directly proportional to the surface area available for gas exchange. The larger the surface area, the more space there is for gas molecules to pass through, which enhances the efficiency of gas exchange. This is why the lungs have a large number of tiny alveoli to maximize the surface area for gas exchange.

Diffusion of respiratory gases is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane through which the gases need to pass. A thinner membrane allows gases to diffuse more rapidly, whereas a thicker membrane creates more resistance and slows down the diffusion process. In the respiratory system, the alveolar-capillary membrane is very thin to facilitate efficient gas exchange.

Diffusion of respiratory gases is directly proportional to the difference in concentration of the gases on either side of the exchange surface. This difference in concentration drives the movement of gases from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration until equilibrium is reached.

The presence of moisture does not directly affect the rate of diffusion of respiratory gases. However, moisture in the respiratory system is essential for keeping the exchange surfaces, such as the alveoli, moist and allowing gases to dissolve for efficient exchange. The presence of moisture prevents the exchange surfaces from drying out and aids in maintaining a favorable environment for gas exchange.

questions.explanation: Explanation: 1. Surface area (Option a): Diffusion of respiratory gases is directly proportional to the surface area available for gas exchange. The larger the surface area, the more space there is for gas molecules to pass through, which enhances the efficiency of gas exchange. This is why the lungs have a large number of tiny alveoli to maximize the surface area for gas exchange. 2. Thickness of the membrane (Option b): Diffusion of respiratory gases is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane through which the gases need to pass. A thinner membrane allows gases to diffuse more rapidly, whereas a thicker membrane creates more resistance and slows down the diffusion process. In the respiratory system, the alveolar-capillary membrane is very thin to facilitate efficient gas exchange. 3. Difference in concentration (Option c): Diffusion of respiratory gases is directly proportional to the difference in concentration of the gases on either side of the exchange surface. This difference in concentration drives the movement of gases from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration until equilibrium is reached. 4. Presence of moisture (Option d): The presence of moisture does not directly affect the rate of diffusion of respiratory gases. However, moisture in the respiratory system is essential for keeping the exchange surfaces, such as the alveoli, moist and allowing gases to dissolve for efficient exchange. The presence of moisture prevents the exchange surfaces from drying out and aids in maintaining a favorable environment for gas exchange.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The body's respiratory centre in the medulla is normally stimulated by an increased concentration of carbon dioxide, and to a lesser extent, by decreased levels of oxygen in arterial blood. Stimulation of the respiratory centre causes an increase in the rate and depth of breathing, thus blowing off excess carbon dioxide and reducing blood acidity.

questions.text: Which one of these shows the path of blood after it leaves the lungs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Pulmonary Vein - Left Atrium - Left Ventricle - Aorta
options.explanation: This is the correct sequence: 1. Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs through the pulmonary vein. 2. The pulmonary vein delivers the oxygen-rich blood to the left atrium of the heart. 3. From the left atrium, the blood flows into the left ventricle and is then pumped out to the body through the aorta, providing oxygen to the tissues.

options.text: Pulmonary Vein - Right Atrium - Right Ventricle - Aorta
options.explanation: This is not the correct sequence. 1. Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs through the pulmonary vein. 2. The pulmonary vein delivers the oxygen-rich blood to the left atrium of the heart. 3. From the left atrium, the blood flows into the left ventricle and is then pumped out to the body through the aorta, providing oxygen to the tissues.

questions.text: All of the following occur due to presence of carbon dioxide in blood except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When there is an increase in carbon dioxide levels in the blood, the body's chemoreceptors detect it and trigger an increase in the rate of breathing to remove excess CO2 from the body.
Carbon dioxide (CO2) in the blood does not directly increase respiratory volume. Instead, it affects the respiratory rate.

Carbon dioxide can combine with water in the blood to form carbonic acid (H2CO3). This reaction lowers the blood's pH, making it more acidic. This acidic environment is essential for maintaining the body's acid-base balance.

options.text: Decreases the oxygen carrying capacity of Hb
options.explanation: High levels of carbon dioxide can lead to a phenomenon called the Bohr effect. In this effect, an increase in CO2 causes hemoglobin (Hb) to have a reduced affinity for oxygen. This means that hemoglobin releases oxygen more readily to the tissues, supporting oxygen exchange in the body.

questions.explanation: Hypercapnia is a build-up of carbon dioxide (CO2) in the blood. It occurs when tiny air sacs in the lungs called alveoli are unable to adequately exchange CO2 from the blood for oxygen.•Increasing the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood therefore results in more H+ ions and a lower pH. However, both these reactions are reversible, and breathing heavily to remove carbon dioxide from the body will lead to a reduction in the concentration of the H+and HCO3- ions, and hence to an increase in pH.

questions.text: Which of the following explains the binding of inorganic component in structure found in erythrocytes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: CO with organic portion of haem
options.explanation: Carbon monoxide (CO) can bind to hemoglobin in a similar manner to oxygen, but it forms a much stronger bond with hemoglobin. This can lead to carbon monoxide poisoning, as it reduces the ability of hemoglobin to bind and transport oxygen effectively. The option is incorrect in saying that it binds to an "organic" portion because it binds to Fe that is inorganic.

options.text: CO2 with NH2 group of haemoglobin
options.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is mainly transported in the blood in the form of bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) rather than directly binding to hemoglobin.

The binding of the inorganic component in the structure found in erythrocytes (red blood cells) involves the binding of oxygen (O2) with the iron (Fe) component of the hemoglobin molecule. Hemoglobin is the protein present in red blood cells that is responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues. When oxygen is inhaled, it diffuses into the blood in the lungs and binds to the iron atoms (Fe) present in the heme groups of hemoglobin. Each hemoglobin molecule can bind up to four oxygen molecules, forming a stable complex called oxyhemoglobin. This oxyhemoglobin complex is then transported by the bloodstream to deliver oxygen to various tissues and organs throughout the body.

options.text: H+ with COOH group of haemoglobin
options.explanation: Hydrogen ions (H+) do not directly bind to hemoglobin. However, they can influence the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve and affect the affinity of hemoglobin for oxygen. The Bohr effect refers to the ability of hydrogen ions (H+) and carbon dioxide (CO2) to influence oxygen release from hemoglobin in response to changes in pH and CO2 concentration.

questions.explanation: •The binding of the inorganic component in the structure found in erythrocytes (red blood cells) involves the binding of oxygen (O2) with the iron (Fe) component of the hemoglobin molecule. Hemoglobin is the protein present in red blood cells that is responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues. •When oxygen is inhaled, it diffuses into the blood in the lungs and binds to the iron atoms (Fe) present in the heme groups of hemoglobin. Each hemoglobin molecule can bind up to four oxygen molecules, forming a stable complex called oxyhemoglobin. This oxyhemoglobin complex is then transported by the bloodstream to deliver oxygen to various tissues and organs throughout the body.

questions.text: Low partial pressure of oxygen in tissues favors _ of oxyheamoglobin.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When the partial pressure of oxygen (PO2) is low in the tissues, it indicates that the tissues are in need of oxygen supply. In this situation, according to the principle of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve, hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen decreases. As a result, the oxyhemoglobin complex readily releases its bound oxygen molecules, allowing the oxygen to diffuse from the blood into the tissues where it is needed for cellular respiration and metabolism. This phenomenon is a crucial aspect of the Bohr effect, which refers to the influence of pH, carbon dioxide (CO2) concentration, and partial pressure of oxygen on hemoglobin's ability to bind and release oxygen. In metabolically active tissues with higher CO2 levels and increased hydrogen ion (H+) concentration (lower pH), the partial pressure of oxygen is lower, and this environment favors the release of oxygen from oxyhemoglobin, enabling efficient oxygen delivery to the tissues. Conversely, in the lungs where oxygen levels are high, hemoglobin readily binds to oxygen, forming oxyhemoglobin for efficient oxygen transport from the lungs to the tissues.

questions.text: Gaseous exchange in animals takes place with the help of process called as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active transport is an energy-consuming process used by cells to move substances against their concentration gradient. It is not the main mechanism for gas exchange in the lungs.
Cyclosis is a process of streaming movement of cytoplasm within cells, mainly seen in certain plant cells. It is not related to gaseous exchange in humans.

Phagocytosis is a process where cells engulf and internalize solid particles, such as bacteria or debris. It is a mechanism used by certain immune cells to eliminate foreign particles, but it is not involved in gaseous exchange.

Gaseous exchange in humans takes place primarily through the process of diffusion. Diffusion is the movement of gases (such as oxygen and carbon dioxide) from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. This process occurs at the respiratory surfaces, which are specialized structures in the lungs known as the alveoli.

questions.explanation: Gaseous exchange in humans takes place primarily through the process of diffusion. Diffusion is the movement of gases (such as oxygen and carbon dioxide) from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. This process occurs at the respiratory surfaces, which are specialized structures in the lungs known as the alveoli.During inhalation, fresh air containing a higher concentration of oxygen is taken into the lungs where it comes into contact with the thin walls of the alveoli. Oxygen diffuses from the alveoli into the surrounding capillaries, which have a lower concentration of oxygen. Simultaneously, carbon dioxide, which has a higher concentration in the capillaries due to cellular metabolism, diffuses from the blood into the alveoli to be exhaled during exhalation.

questions.text: The characteristics red color of haemoglobin is due to:

options.explanation: This option is correct. The characteristic red color of hemoglobin is primarily due to the presence of the haem group, which contains an iron atom that can bind to oxygen. When oxygen binds to the iron atom in the haem group, it causes a conformational change in hemoglobin, giving it a bright red color.
The color of hemoglobin is primarily determined by the haem group and its iron atom, rather than the specific globin chains (alpha or beta).

options.text: It is dipeptide and present in RBC
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. Hemoglobin is not a dipeptide; it is a complex protein composed of four polypeptide chains (two alpha and two beta chains) along with haem groups. While hemoglobin is present in RBCs, it is not accurately described as a dipeptide.

options.text: It is dipeptide in mammals and localized in RBC
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. Hemoglobin is not a dipeptide, and its structure is not accurately described as being composed of only two amino acids. Additionally, while hemoglobin is present in RBCs, it is not accurate to describe it as localized exclusively in RBCs.

options.text: It is present in dissolved state in blood plasma
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. Hemoglobin is primarily found within red blood cells and is not normally present in significant amounts in the dissolved state in blood plasma.
