questions.text: Arteries are best defined as the vessels which:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Carry blood away from the heart to different organs.
options.explanation: Arteries are best defined as the vessels which carry blood away from the heart. They have thick walls and are elastic, which allows them to withstand the pressure of the blood being pumped by the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller vessels as they get further away from the heart.

options.text: Break up into capillaries which reunite to form a vein.
options.explanation: Arteries break up into capillaries, which are the smallest blood vessels in the body. Capillaries are so small that red blood cells can only pass through them one at a time. Capillaries allow the exchange of oxygen and nutrients between the blood and the tissues. The capillaries then reunite to form veins, which carry blood back to the heart.

options.text: Carry blood from one visceral organ to another visceral organ.
options.explanation: Visceral arteries branch off of the abdominal aorta and supply blood to the stomach, intestines, liver, spleen, pancreas, and kidneys.

options.text: Supply oxygenated blood to different organs.
options.explanation: The arteries that supply oxygenated blood to different organs include the coronary arteries, pulmonary arteries, cerebral arteries, carotid arteries, subclavian arteries, abdominal aorta, and femoral arteries.

questions.explanation: Arteries are best defined as the vessels which carry blood away from the heart. They have thick walls and are elastic, which allows them to withstand the pressure of the blood being pumped by the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller vessels as they get further away from the heart. The smallest arteries are called arterioles.

options.explanation: The closing of the atrioventricular (AV) valves is associated with a sound called S1, which is also known as the first heart sound. S1 is a high-pitched, sharp sound that is often heard in the left side of the chest.

The opening of the atrioventricular (AV) valves is not associated with a sound. This is because the AV valves open when the pressure in the atria is higher than the pressure in the ventricles. This is a very rapid process, and there is not enough time for the valves to create a vibration that can be heard.

The closure of the semilunar valves is associated with the second heart sound, which is also known as the dub. The semilunar valves are located at the base of the heart, where the heart's chambers connect to the aorta and pulmonary artery.

The opening of the semilunar valves is not associated with a sound. This is because the semilunar valves open when the pressure in the ventricles is lower than the pressure in the arteries. This is a very rapid process, and there is not enough time for the valves to create a vibration that can be heard.

options.explanation: The root hairs are separated from the soil by a permeable membrane. The permeable membrane is made up of a layer of cells that are joined together by tiny pores. These pores are small enough to allow water and nutrients to pass through, but they are too small for larger molecules, such as salts, to pass through. The permeable membrane is an important part of the root hair's ability to absorb water and nutrients from the soil. Without the permeable membrane, the root hairs would not be able to take up the water and nutrients that the plant needs to survive.

Endodermis: The water molecules then pass through the endodermis, which is a layer of cells that surrounds the vascular tissue. Pericycle: The water molecules then move to the pericycle, which is a layer of cells that surrounds the xylem. Protoxylem: The water molecules then move into the protoxylem, which is the oldest part of the xylem. Metaxylem: The water molecules then move into the metaxylem, which is the youngest part of the xylem. The water molecules are transported up the xylem by a process called capillary action. Capillary action is the ability of water to move up narrow tubes against the force of gravity. options.text: Solute particles from higher concentration to lower concentration
options.explanation: Solute particles will always move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. This process is called diffusion.

questions.text: The lymphatic system consists of all the following except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blood is not part of the lymphatic system because it has different functions and components. Blood is a fluid that circulates through the body, carrying oxygen and nutrients to the cells and removing waste products. It is made up of plasma, red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. The lymphatic system is a network of vessels that helps to remove excess fluid and waste products from the body. It also helps to fight infection. The lymphatic system is made up of lymph, lymph nodes, and lymph vessels.
Lymph vessels: These are thin-walled vessels that carry lymph throughout the body.
Lymph nodes: These are small, bean-shaped organs that are found throughout the body. They filter lymph and produce white blood cells.

questions.text: Inferior vena cava receives blood from all of the following except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The external iliac veins are blood vessels in your pelvis. Your pelvis is the part of your skeletal system between your lower torso (trunk) and legs. It sits just above where your legs and hips connect. Your external iliac veins send blood that no longer has oxygen from your lower body back to your heart to get oxygen.

The inferior vena cava (IVC) does not receive blood directly from the hepatic portal vein because the hepatic portal vein first drains into the liver. The liver is responsible for filtering and processing the blood from the digestive tract before it returns to the general circulation. If the IVC received blood directly from the hepatic portal vein, this would bypass the liver and the blood would not be filtered or processed. This could lead to problems, such as liver disease.

The renal veins are blood vessels that return blood to the heart from the kidney. Each kidney is drained by its own renal vein (the right and left renal vein). Each renal vein drains into a large vein called the inferior vena cava (IVC), which carries blood directly to the heart.

Hepatic veins are blood vessels that return low-oxygen blood from your liver back to the heart. The veins are key players in the supply chain that moves the blood that delivers nutrients and oxygen to every cell in your body. A blockage in one of the hepatic veins may damage your liver.

questions.explanation: The inferior vena cava (IVC) does not receive blood directly from the hepatic portal vein because the hepatic portal vein first drains into the liver. The liver is responsible for filtering and processing the blood from the digestive tract before it returns to the general circulation. questions.text: Outlet valves of heart are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and the right ventricle. It has three leaflets, which are called cusps. The cusps prevent blood from flowing back into the right atrium when the right ventricle contracts.

The bicuspid valve is also known as the aortic valve. It is located between the left ventricle and the aorta. It has two leaflets, which are called cusps. The cusps prevent blood from flowing back into the left ventricle when the left ventricle contracts.

The outlet valves of the heart are called semilunar valves. They are located at the base of the aorta and the pulmonary artery. The semilunar valves prevent blood from flowing back into the ventricles after it has been pumped out of the heart.

The mitral valve is located between the left atrium and the left ventricle. It has two leaflets, which are called cusps. The cusps prevent blood from flowing back into the left atrium when the left ventricle contracts.

questions.explanation: The outlet valves of the heart are called semilunar valves. They are located at the base of the aorta and the pulmonary artery. The semilunar valves prevent blood from flowing back into the ventricles after it has been pumped out of the heart.

questions.text: Elastic fibers and muscles are absent in?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Veins and arteries are larger blood vessels that carry blood away from and towards the heart. They have elastic fibers and muscles, which allow them to expand and contract. This helps to regulate blood flow and blood pressure.

Veins and arteries are larger blood vessels that carry blood away from and towards the heart. They have elastic fibers and muscles, which allow them to expand and contract. This helps to regulate blood flow and blood pressure.

Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels in the body. They are only about one cell thick, and they allow nutrients and oxygen to pass from the blood to the cells. Capillaries do not have elastic fibers or muscles, so they cannot expand or contract. This is why blood pressure is lower in capillaries than in veins and arteries.

questions.text: The flow of lymph is always
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The flow of lymph is always unidirectional, meaning that it flows in one direction only. This is because the lymphatic system is a one-way system. Lymph flows from the tissues, through the lymphatic vessels, and eventually back into the bloodstream. The flow of lymph is always towards the thoracic duct. The thoracic duct is the largest lymphatic vessel in the body. It is located in the chest and it drains lymph from the upper body, including the arms, head, and neck. The lymph then flows into the bloodstream through the subclavian vein.

The thoracic duct drains the lower extremities, pelvis, abdomen, left side of the thorax, left upper extremity, and left side of the head and neck. It originates at the cisterna chyli (when present) and terminates at the junction of the left subclavian and left internal jugular veins.

Adenoids are a patch of tissue that is high up in the throat, just behind the nose. They, along with the tonsils, are part of the lymphatic system. The lymphatic system clears away infection and keeps body fluids in balance. The adenoids and tonsils work by trapping germs coming in through the mouth and nose.
The axilla is an anatomical region under the shoulder joint where the arm connects to the shoulder.

questions.text: The pressure within capillaries causes a continuous leakage of fluid from the blood plasma into the spaces that surround the capillaries and tissue. This fluid is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lymph is a clear, colorless fluid that is found in the lymphatic system. It is made up of water, proteins, electrolytes, and other dissolved substances. Lymph is important for transporting nutrients and oxygen to the cells, and for removing waste products from the cells. It also helps to fight infection.

The fluid that leaks from the blood plasma into the spaces that surround the capillaries and tissue is known as interstitial fluid. Interstitial fluid is a clear, watery fluid that bathes the cells of the body. It contains nutrients, oxygen, and waste products that are exchanged between the blood and the cells.

Intracellular fluid is the fluid that is found inside the cells. It is made up of water, proteins, electrolytes, and other dissolved substances. Intracellular fluid is important for maintaining the cell's environment and for carrying out the cell's functions.

Serum is the clear, yellowish fluid that remains after blood has been clotted and the cells have been removed. It is made up of water, proteins, electrolytes, and other dissolved substances. Serum is important for transporting nutrients and oxygen to the cells, and for removing waste products from the cells.

questions.text: Trypsinogen is converted into trypsin by the activity of:

options.explanation: Oxyntic cells are found in the stomach and produce hydrochloric acid (HCl), which is essential for the digestion of food. HCl helps to break down proteins and other molecules in food, making them easier to digest.

Absorptive cells are found in the small intestine and are responsible for absorbing nutrients from food. They have microvilli, which are tiny projections that increase the surface area of the cells, allowing them to absorb more nutrients.

Trypsinogen is converted into trypsin by the action of enterokinase, an enzyme that is produced by the small intestine. Enterokinase is activated by the presence of food in the small intestine, and it then activates trypsinogen, which converts it into trypsin.

Peptidase is an enzyme that breaks down proteins into smaller molecules, such as amino acids. It is produced by the pancreas and is found in the small intestine. Peptidase helps to complete the digestion of proteins, making them available for absorption by the body.

questions.text: In large intestine bacteria synthesize some vitamins especially:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The synthesis of Vitamin A does not primarily occur in the large intestine. Instead, Vitamin A is a fat-soluble vitamin that is obtained from the diet, particularly from foods containing retinoids (preformed Vitamin A) and carotenoids (provitamin A). These compounds are found in various animal-derived products (e.g., liver, eggs, dairy) and colorful fruits and vegetables (e.g., carrots, spinach, sweet potatoes). Once ingested, Vitamin A is absorbed in the small intestine and then transported to various tissues in the body where it plays essential roles in vision, immune function, and cellular differentiation.

Like Vitamin A, the synthesis of Vitamin E does not occur in the large intestine. Vitamin E is a fat-soluble vitamin that acts as an antioxidant, protecting cells from oxidative damage. It is obtained from the diet, mainly from vegetable oils, nuts, seeds, and leafy green vegetables. After ingestion, Vitamin E is absorbed in the small intestine and then distributed throughout the body's tissues.

Vitamin D is unique because it can be synthesized in the skin upon exposure to ultraviolet B (UVB) radiation from sunlight. It is also obtained from certain dietary sources, such as fatty fish (e.g., salmon, mackerel) and fortified foods like milk and cereals. In the skin, 7-dehydrocholesterol, a precursor molecule, is converted to Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) through UVB exposure. After synthesis in the skin or ingestion from the diet, Vitamin D undergoes further metabolic conversions in the liver and kidneys to its active form, calcitriol. This active form regulates calcium and phosphate metabolism, promoting bone health and supporting immune function.

The bacteria in the large intestine synthesize some vitamins, especially vitamin K Vitamin K: Vitamin K is important for blood clotting. It is also involved in the metabolism of calcium and bone health.

options.explanation: Peristalsis does not occur in the oral cavity. The oral cavity is the first part of the digestive tract, and it is where food is chewed and mixed with saliva. In the small intestine, peristalsis helps to mix food with digestive juices and move it along the digestive tract. The small intestine is responsible for the majority of nutrient absorption, so it is important that food is moved through it quickly and efficiently. Peristalsis helps to ensure that food is not left in the small intestine for too long, which would allow nutrients to be absorbed too slowly.

In the large intestine, peristalsis is less important for moving food along the digestive tract. The large intestine is responsible for absorbing water and some nutrients from food, and it does not need to move food as quickly as the small intestine. Instead, peristalsis in the large intestine helps to mix food and waste products, and it helps to push waste products out of the body.

questions.text: Mucous membranes are part of body defense system and they offer:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mucous membranes are a type of epithelial tissue that lines the inside of many organs, including the respiratory tract, digestive tract, and genitourinary tract. They are part of the body's first line of defense against infection, and they offer physical barriers in several ways: The mucus layer: Mucus is a thick, sticky substance that is produced by the cells of the mucous membrane. It helps to trap bacteria and viruses, preventing them from reaching the underlying tissues. The tight junctions: The cells of the mucous membrane are held together by tight junctions, which are small gaps between the cells. These tight junctions help to prevent bacteria and viruses from passing between the cells. The cilia: The cells of the mucous membrane also have cilia, which are tiny hair-like projections. The cilia move in a coordinated fashion to help move the mucus layer along the surface of the mucous membrane. This helps to remove bacteria and viruses from the surface of the mucous membrane.
Mechanical barriers physically prevent pathogens from entering the body.
Chemical barriers kill or inhibit the growth of pathogens.

Biological barriers are made up of beneficial bacteria that live on the skin and in the mucous membranes. These bacteria help to prevent the growth of harmful bacteria by competing for nutrients and by producing antimicrobial substances.

questions.text: The nodules of lymphoid tissue found in the wall of the intestinal tract are termed as:

options.explanation: There is no recognized medical term or anatomical structure known as "Grave's region" in the context of lymphoid tissue or the intestinal tract. Grave's disease, on the other hand, is an autoimmune disorder that affects the thyroid gland.

Peyer's patches are nodules of lymphoid tissue found in the wall of the small intestine. They are part of the gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) and play a crucial role in immune defense and monitoring the intestinal contents for harmful microorganisms.

questions.explanation: Zymogen cells, also known as chief cells, secrete pepsinogen and chymosin. Pepsinogen is an inactive form of pepsin, which is an enzyme that breaks down proteins. Chymosin is an enzyme that curdles milk. Both pepsinogen and chymosin are stored in the stomach in an inactive form. When they come into contact with hydrochloric acid, they are activated and begin to digest food.

questions.text: It is not produced by duodenum:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cholecystokinin is produced by the duodenum. It is a hormone that is released in response to the presence of fatty acids and amino acids in the small intestine.
Secretin is produced by the duodenum. Secretin is a hormone that is released in response to the presence of acidic chyme in the duodenum.

Mucus is secreted by the duodenum. Mucus is a thick, slippery substance that helps to protect the lining of the duodenum from the harsh acids and enzymes that are present in the digestive juices. It also helps to lubricate the duodenum, making it easier for food to pass through.
Biliverdin is not produced by the duodenum. Biliverdin is a green pigment that is formed during the breakdown of heme, which is a component of hemoglobin.

questions.explanation: Out of all the options mentioned above, biliverdin is the only one that is not produced by duodenum. It is a green pigment that is produced during the breakdown of haeme, a component of haemoglobin.

questions.text: Salivary amylase acts on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Salivary amylase is secreted by salivary glands present in the buccal cavity. It is an enzyme that acts on starch, a type of carbohydrate and breaks it down into maltose, a disaccharide.
Amylase does not attack cellulose due to its helical structure. The monomers in cellulose are bonded together differently than in starch.
Salivary amylase does not act on protein as it does not have the necessary 3D structure to calatyze the breakdown of proteins.
Salivary amylase cannot break the bonds present in lipids, so it cannot digest it.

questions.explanation: Salivary amylase is secreted by salivary glands present in the buccal cavity. It is an enzyme that acts on starch, a type of carbohydrate and breaks it down into maltose, a disaccharide. It does not act on cellulose, protein or lipids as the bonds present in these polymers are different to those that hold the monomers in starch.

questions.text: Cells that lower pH of stomach:

options.explanation: Mucus is secreted by the mucous glands and goblet cells. It is neutral and does not lower the pH of the stomach.
Cheif cells also known as zymogen cells secrete inactive enzymes necessary for breakdown of protein. This does not lower the pH of the stomach.
Zymogen cells secrete inactive enzymes necessary for breakdown of protein. This does not lower the pH of the stomach.
Parietal cells are found in the stomach lining. They secrete HCl, which is a strong acid and creates an acidic medium in the stomach (lowers the pH of the stomach).

questions.text: Erypsin works on:

questions.text: After stomach digestion occurs in:

Cecum is a pouch like structure in the large intestine. It is the place where microbial digestion of soluble or insoluble carbohydrates occur.

questions.text: Which is true about pepsin?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is produced in inactivated form
options.explanation: Pepsin is a protease which is produced in its inactive form as pepsinogen by the chief cells. HCl present in the stomach activates pepsinogen to pepsin which then breaks down large protein.
Pepsin is produced in its inactive form by zymogen cells present in the stomach walls.

questions.text: Secretion of secretin is forced by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The secretion of secretin is forced by the presence of acidic chyme in the duodenum. Chyme is the partially digested food that enters the small intestine from the stomach. When chyme enters the duodenum, it triggers the release of secretin from S cells in the duodenal mucosa. Secretin is a hormone that stimulates the secretion of bicarbonate from the pancreas.

Bile is a yellow-green fluid that is produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder. It is released into the small intestine when food enters the duodenum. Its main function is to emulsify fat into fatty acid and glycerol. It is not responsible for the secretion of secretin.

Pancreatic juice is a fluid that is produced by the pancreas and released into the small intestine. It contains a number of enzymes that help to digest carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. It is not responsible for the secretion of secretin.
Only food or chyme in the small intestine from the stomach promotes the secretion of secretin.

questions.text: Botulism is severe form of:

Constipation is characterized by infrequent bowel movements, hard stools, and difficulty passing stools.

questions.text: Loss of weight takes place due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Anorexia nervosa is an eating disorder characterized by a refusal to maintain a healthy body weight, an intense fear of gaining weight, and a distorted body image. People with anorexia nervosa often restrict their food intake, engage in excessive exercise, or purge after eating resulting in decline of body weight.

Bulimia nervosa is an eating disorder that causes you to eat large amounts of food at one time (binge) and then get rid of it (purge). Sufficient nutrition is not provided to the body which causes a decline in the body weight.
Both anorexia nervosa and bulimia nervosa are eating disorders that lead to a decline in body weight.
Constipation is a common problem that affects people of all ages. It is characterized by infrequent bowel movements, hard stools, and difficulty passing stools.

questions.text: Which enzyme is found in saliva?

questions.explanation: The major enzyme present in saliva is salivary amylase. Ptyalin is a form of salivary amylase. Saliva does contain contain lipase, however, it is present in very small amounts. It does not contain pepsin or lactase.

questions.text: Pair of salivary glands located behind the jaws is called:

options.explanation: The sublingual gland is one of the three major salivary glands in the human body. It is located under the tongue, in front of the submaxillary gland. The sublingual gland is the smallest of the three major salivary glands, but it is still responsible for producing about 15% of the total volume of saliva produced by the salivary glands.

The pair of salivary glands located behind the jaws is called the submaxillary glands. They are the largest of the three pairs of major salivary glands, and they produce about 70% of the total volume of saliva produced by the salivary glands.

The parotid glands are the largest of the three major salivary glands in the human body. They are located in front of and below the ears, and they produce about 30% of the total volume of saliva produced by the salivary glands.
The adrenal glands (also known as suprarenal glands) sit atop the kidneys. They are part of the endocrine system, which is a collection of glands that produce hormones.

questions.text: Which of the following would most greatly increase the activity of an enzyme functioning in the small intestine?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Decreasing the temperature would slow down the reaction because enzymes are proteins and proteins are denatured by heat. Denaturation is the process of changing the shape of a protein, which can make it unable to function properly.
Increasing the amount of substrate would only increase the reaction rate to a certain point. Once all of the enzymes are bound to substrate molecules, the reaction rate will plateau.
Decreasing the pH would denature the enzyme, making it inactive. Enzymes have an optimal pH range, and outside of this range, they will be denatured.

Increasing the number of enzymes is the best way to increase enzyme activity is because enzymes are catalysts, and they work by binding to their substrate and then catalyzing a reaction. The more enzymes that are present, the more substrate molecules can be bound and catalyzed, which leads to an increase in the reaction rate.

questions.text: In the intestine the branches of lymph capillaries within villi are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lacteals are specialized lymph capillaries that are found in the villi of the small intestine. They are responsible for absorbing fats and fat-soluble vitamins from the digestive tract and transporting them to the lymphatic system.
Lymph is the fluid that flows through the lymphatic system. It is a clear, colorless fluid that contains white blood cells, proteins, and other substances
. Lymphatic vessels are the tubes that carry lymph throughout the body. They are similar to blood vessels, but they have thinner walls and contain valves that prevent the lymph from flowing backward.

Lymph nodes are small, bean-shaped organs that are found throughout the body. They are located along the lymphatic vessels and filter lymph. They also produce white blood cells that help to fight infection.

questions.text: Bacteria live in human body for enzymatic source and vitamin:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Enterococci are a group of Gram-positive bacteria that are found in the human body, including the mouth, gut, and skin. They are known for their ability to produce a variety of enzymes, including enzymes that are involved in the breakdown of food and the synthesis of vitamins.
Pseudomonas are a group of Gram-negative bacteria that are found in soil, water, and the environment. They are not typically found in the human body, but they can sometimes cause infections.
Campylobacter are a group of Gram-negative bacteria that are found in the gut of animals, including poultry and cattle. They can cause food poisoning in humans.

Spirochetes are a group of spiral-shaped bacteria that are found in the environment and in the bodies of animals, including humans. They can cause a variety of diseases, including Lyme disease and syphilis.

questions.text: The semi solid mass in stomach is known as:

Incomplete or imperfect digestion is known as dyspepsia. Dyspepsia is a medical condition characterized by upper abdominal pain, fullness, and discomfort.
